_meta.note: Chase values are a June 2026 snapshot — treat as stale after ~a month.

sv.why: The definitive modern collector set: an all-Eeveelution theme, the first-ever Master Ball reverse-holo parallels (which make even commons worth keeping), God Packs, and the era's most coveted card in Umbreon ex. Universal appeal plus friendlier-than-usual SIR odds lock in #1.
sv.econ: Average box pull value is ~$90–150 unless you hit Umbreon, so the math favors the house — and sealed is scarce and pricey. Rip for the Eevee experience; buy specific SIRs as singles.

sv.why: Pure Kanto nostalgia where every one of the original 151 is worth collecting, wrapped in gorgeous Illustration Rares and anchored by a strong Charizard ex chase. Endlessly reprinted, so it stays accessible.
sv.econ: Reprints keep boxes affordable and genuinely rip-worthy for the IR run. Grab the Charizard ex as a single rather than chasing it through packs.

sv.why: The single most fun set to open — shinies land roughly 1 in 3–4 packs, so nearly every pack hits. Shiny Mew and Shiny Charizard headline. It sits just below S-tier only because most shinies are individually cheap and there are no booster boxes.
sv.econ: The best pure-rip set on the board; ETBs and bundles deliver constant shiny dopamine. Only the two big shiny SIRs are worth buying as singles.

sv.why: Team Rocket's return gives it the deepest, most characterful chase tier of the era — Mewtwo, Cynthia's Garchomp, Ethan's Ho-Oh — plus a strong IR run. Huge crossover appeal.
sv.econ: Boxes are rip-worthy for the character IR/SIR spread; cherry-pick the marquee Rocket SIRs as singles.

sv.ratenote: every Unova mon gets a hit
sv.why: A Gen-5 Unova love letter: every Unova Pokémon gets a special-art version, a brand-new BWR rarity debuts, and pull rates are generous. An enormous, joyful collecting playground.
sv.econ: Generous pulls make ripping genuinely rewarding, so packs are great here; the monochrome BWR Zekrom and Reshiram are the singles to target.

sv.why: Widely considered the second-best standard set behind Prismatic, carried by the Pikachu ex chase and strong dual player/collector appeal. In-print and accessible.
sv.econ: Still rippable at retail for the IR/SIR spread; buy Pikachu ex as a single.

sv.why: The most balanced standard set of the era — a strong Greninja ex chase, the beloved Bloodmoon Ursaluna, and meta-defining Ogerpon cards keep both players and collectors happy.
sv.econ: Solid rip value; pick up Greninja ex and the alt-art trainers (Perrin, Carmine) as singles.

sv.why: Carried by the iconic Iono SIR and a surprise alt-art Magikarp that became one of the era's priciest non-chase cards. Deep set, but early-era pull rates are rough.
sv.econ: Older and rough to rip — buy the Magikarp IR and the Iono SIR as singles.

sv.why: Debuted the Trainer's Pokémon mechanic with a meta-defining Lillie's Clefairy chase and lots of character cards, but many trainer Pokémon underwhelmed and singles stayed cheap.
sv.econ: Singles are affordable across the board; rip a few packs for fun, then finish on the secondary market.

sv.why: A solid foundation with a standout Gardevoir ex and a surprisingly deep IR lineup, but early-era rough rates and fewer headline cards keep it mid-pack.
sv.econ: Rotating out and cheap as singles — just buy the cards you want.

sv.why: Some of the most beautiful cards of the era (Roaring Moon, Iron Valiant, the Groudon IR) trapped behind notoriously brutal pull rates — gorgeous to own, miserable to rip.
sv.econ: Do not rip for value; the bad odds make singles the only sane route.

sv.why: A compact special set with a decent concentration of hits, but a low chase ceiling keeps the excitement modest.
sv.econ: Small and cheap to complete via singles; ripping is fine but unremarkable.

sv.why: Frustrating on both ends: no true chase (its Charizard ex has been alt-art reprinted to death) and some of the era's worst SIR rates.
sv.econ: Skip ripping; buy the few cards you want as singles.

sv.why: Meta-relevant in its day but forgettable for collectors, with weak chase appeal and little nostalgia.
sv.econ: Cheap singles; no real reason to rip.

sv.why: Nice Squirtle and Bulbasaur Illustration Rares, but Terapagos never gained traction, leaving it the thinnest set of the era for collectors.
sv.econ: In-print and cheap — buy the Kanto-starter IRs as singles.

me.why: The era's chase powerhouse: it holds the three most valuable English cards of 2026 (Mega Gengar, Pikachu and Mega Dragonite SIRs), debuts the stunning kanji-art Mega Attack Rare tier, and has God Packs stuffed with 3 MARs + 7 SIRs. It also has the friendliest confirmed pull rates of the era.
me.econ: It's a special set — no booster boxes, only ETBs and bundles, and the 575+ card master set is brutal to finish. Chases run into four figures, so buy singles; rip ETBs for the God-pack lottery and the art.

me.why: The foundational debut of the Mega era — the full ten-type Mega ex roster, new-series prestige, and the strongest launch since Sword & Shield base. It ranks high on importance even though chases have cooled and pull rates are tight (SIR 1:101, MHR 1:1,260).
me.econ: An Enhanced box at MSRP (~$180–200) is a fine rip for the Bulbasaur promo plus commons (pack EV ~$6); buy the Lucario/Gardevoir MHRs and SIRs as singles.

me.why: The newest set rides a huge Mega Greninja chase (SIR ~$490, gold MHR ~$440) with solid SIR depth ($55–109) and a fan-favorite Kalos theme. Prices are still settling and it leans heavily on Greninja, but it's a genuinely exciting rip right now.
me.econ: Boxes are available and rip-worthy for the SIR spread; Greninja is the single to buy. Its live chase actually tops ME01's, so it's reasonable to rank these two either way.

me.why: The era's smallest regular set and the definition of a one-card set: the Mega Charizard X SIR (~$750) and nostalgia carry it, while non-Charizard SIRs sit under $35.
me.econ: Boxes near MSRP make it the best sealed value to rip if you just want to open — but EV outside the Zard is thin, so buy Charizard as a single.

me.why: The completionist's pick: a small, one-binder master set, a generous IR rate (~1 in 18), and a Z-A video-game tie-in pulling in new collectors. Only the era's lowest chase ceiling (~$187 top card) holds it back.
me.econ: The most rip-friendly Mega set, and cheap to finish via singles either way — a great low-stakes set to open.
